methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $26M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $1.8B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Watershed
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $1.8B

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Terraform Industries is a Series A climate and energy company that has raised $26M to date. Valuation analysis relies on a peer set of four same-stage, same-sector private companies (Watershed, Graphyte, Fourth Power, and Steady Energy) alongside stage-progression methodology. The broader Climate & Energy sector comprises one public comparable and eight private peers.

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression, using total capital raised as a proxy baseline, applies a 2.0x multiplier typical of Series A advancement, yielding $52M. The peer-median approach references four direct comparables with valuations ranging from $110M (Steady Energy) to $1.8B (Watershed), producing a median of $175M. The dispersion reflects varying technology maturity and commercial stage within the cohort.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $44M to $201M, with a median point of $114M. The gap between stage-progression and peer-median results reflects uncertainty regarding where Terraform sits relative to peers on progress metrics—capital efficiency, technical risk, or path to revenue. The $1.8B outlier (Watershed) indicates ceiling risk if the company reaches comparable technical or commercial milestones, but also highlights that peer comparability assumptions remain the primary driver of variance. Capital structure, burn rate, and product development stage relative to peers would sharpen this range materially.
